name: FRC Strategy History Starter Pack
description: A public starter benchmark pack for FRC game knowledge, strategy metas, alliance selection logic, scouting interpretation, and historical season reasoning. Most tasks are intentionally unverified and should be reviewed by the community before being treated as canonical.

tasks.prompt: A 2019-style alliance loses despite having two robots with strong scoring bursts. Match notes say traffic and unclear lane priority caused repeated dead time. What should the drive team review before the next match?
tasks.expectedAnswer: A good answer should review pathing, lane priority, role assignment, communication, queueing near scoring locations, defensive pressure, and fallback plans when a partner is blocked.

rubric.point: Mentions pathing, traffic, or lane priority
Mentions role clarity or communication
Mentions defense, fallback plans, or blocked-partner contingencies

tasks.sourceNote: For 2019 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For match-analysis tasks, verify field and scoring terminology in the official manual. Use The Blue Alliance match videos/data or team review resources when making concrete event claims.
tasks.publicExplanation: Tests whether the model can diagnose match flow instead of only saying to score faster.

tasks.prompt: In a 2019 playoff picklist discussion, your alliance already has two high-output scorers. The next choice is between a third scorer with reliability issues and a lower-scoring robot known for disciplined defense. Explain how you would decide.
tasks.expectedAnswer: Compare marginal scoring need, reliability, defensive value, foul risk, matchup needs, partner compatibility, and repairability. The answer should not pick only by average points.

rubric.point: Compares marginal role fit rather than raw average score only
Considers reliability, repairability, or failure risk
Considers defense, foul risk, or opponent matchup

tasks.sourceNote: For 2019 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For alliance-selection claims, verify game constraints in official materials and support meta claims with event/picklist evidence from The Blue Alliance, Statbotics, Chief Delphi recaps, or public team strategy resources.

tasks.prompt: A scout report says Team A has the same scoring average as Team B, but Team A has much lower variance and fewer fouls. Give one reason Team A may be more valuable in playoffs.
tasks.expectedAnswer: Lower variance and fewer fouls can make Team A more predictable in tight playoff matches, reducing downside risk even if the scoring average is similar.

rubric.point: Mentions consistency or lower variance
Mentions foul or penalty risk
Connects the issue to playoff or elimination reliability

tasks.sourceNote: For 2019 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For scouting-interpretation tasks, verify game terms in official materials and support data claims with The Blue Alliance, Statbotics, team scouting resources, or clearly scoped community analysis.
tasks.publicExplanation: Tests whether the model understands variance and risk in picklist decisions.

tasks.prompt: A benchmark question asks for an exact 2019 protected-zone contact ruling, but the official manual excerpt is not provided. What is the best answer style?
tasks.expectedAnswer: C
choices: A. Guess the rule from memory and cite a made-up rule number
B. Avoid the rule issue and discuss drivetrain design
C. State that the exact ruling needs verification from the official manual or updates before giving a definitive answer
D. Assume all protected-zone contact has the same penalty across seasons

rubric.point: Selects C

tasks.sourceNote: Verify against the 2019 DESTINATION: DEEP SPACE Game Manual, official team updates, and Q&A. Check exact protected-zone/contact terminology, penalties, and any cited rule numbers before marking source_verified. This task is community-reviewed as a source-discipline item: it does not assert the exact official rule answer, and should remain source_verified only after reviewers attach exact manual, update, or Q&A citations for any season-specific rule content.
tasks.publicExplanation: Tests source discipline for official rule questions.

sources.title: Official FRC Competition Manual and Q&A System
sources.note: Placeholder source location. Community reviewers should link the exact 2019 manual section or update.

tasks.prompt: A team can build a narrow mechanism optimized for one common scoring role or a broader mechanism that is slower but more flexible. What tradeoffs should they analyze?
tasks.expectedAnswer: They should compare strategic value, cycle time, reliability, driver practice, integration risk, partner compatibility, event variability, and maintenance complexity.

rubric.point: Mentions strategic value or role specialization
Mentions cycle time or reliability
Mentions partner compatibility, practice, integration, or maintenance

tasks.sourceNote: For 2019 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For design-tradeoff tasks, verify game-objective terminology in the official manual. Support meta/design claims with team technical resources, public build blogs, event data, or community discussion when available.
tasks.publicExplanation: Tests engineering strategy under build-season constraints.

tasks.prompt: In a 2020-style qualification match, an alliance can chase a ranking objective or play a safer plan that likely wins the match. What factors should the strategist weigh?
tasks.expectedAnswer: A good answer weighs win probability, ranking-objective probability, current ranking needs, alliance capabilities, opponent strength, execution risk, and time remaining.

rubric.point: Compares win probability with ranking-objective probability
Considers alliance and opponent capabilities
Considers execution risk, time remaining, or event ranking context

tasks.sourceNote: Verify against the 2020 INFINITE RECHARGE Game Manual, official team updates, and Q&A. Check exact rule language, ranking-point objectives, penalties, and any season-specific terminology before marking source_verified. For ranking-point tasks, check the exact season manual and official updates for ranking-point objectives and thresholds before marking source_verified.

tasks.prompt: A team with limited time can either improve scoring accuracy or add a complex endgame mechanism. What should they analyze before choosing?
tasks.expectedAnswer: They should estimate point impact, reliability, integration risk, practice time, schedule risk, repairability, and alliance value.

rubric.point: Mentions expected point or match impact
Mentions reliability or integration risk
Mentions practice, schedule, repairability, or alliance value

tasks.sourceNote: For 2020 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For design-tradeoff tasks, verify game-objective terminology in the official manual. Support meta/design claims with team technical resources, public build blogs, event data, or community discussion when available.

tasks.prompt: Return JSON with keys "pickRisk", "strengths", "concerns", and "dataToVerify" for a robot with strong teleop output, inconsistent autonomous, and occasional mechanical downtime.
tasks.expectedAnswer: {"pickRisk":"medium","strengths":["teleop scoring"],"concerns":["inconsistent autonomous","mechanical downtime"],"dataToVerify":["auto success rate","downtime causes","cycle consistency","repair history"]}

rubric.point: Uses requested keys
Identifies teleop output as a strength
Identifies autonomous inconsistency or downtime as concerns

tasks.sourceNote: For 2020 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For scouting-interpretation tasks, verify game terms in official materials and support data claims with The Blue Alliance, Statbotics, team scouting resources, or clearly scoped community analysis.

tasks.prompt: If a 2020 rule question depends on a manual section that is not included in the prompt, what should a model do?
tasks.expectedAnswer: B
choices: A. Give a precise rule number from memory
B. Say the exact answer needs the official manual or update and avoid inventing details
C. Assume the 2024 rule is the same
D. Answer only with team preference

rubric.point: Selects B

tasks.sourceNote: Verify against the 2020 INFINITE RECHARGE Game Manual, official team updates, and Q&A. Check exact rule language, ranking-point objectives, penalties, and any season-specific terminology before marking source_verified. This task is community-reviewed as a source-discipline item: it does not assert the exact official rule answer, and should remain source_verified only after reviewers attach exact manual, update, or Q&A citations for any season-specific rule content.
tasks.publicExplanation: Tests refusal to hallucinate rule text.

sources.title: Official FRC Competition Manual and Q&A System
sources.note: Placeholder source location. Community reviewers should link the exact 2020 manual section or update.

tasks.prompt: When comparing 2020 and 2021 model performance, why should an FRC benchmark be careful about historical context and event availability?
tasks.expectedAnswer: A good answer should note that event schedules and available match data were unusual, community analysis may be incomplete, and comparisons should separate official season rules from limited-event observations and later retrospective claims.

rubric.point: Mentions unusual event availability or limited match data
Distinguishes official rules from community or retrospective meta
Warns against overgeneralizing from small samples

tasks.sourceNote: For 2020 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For historical-meta tasks, separate official season facts from retrospective community interpretation. Use official materials for rules and TBA/Statbotics/Chief Delphi/team resources for event or meta context.

tasks.prompt: A model compares 2021 at-home challenge performance directly to normal in-person event performance. What concerns should it raise?
tasks.expectedAnswer: It should discuss format differences, self-reported or nonstandardized conditions, limited defense and interaction data, different strategic incentives, and the need to avoid direct apples-to-apples comparisons.

rubric.point: Mentions format or field-condition differences
Mentions missing defense, interaction, or live-match context
Warns against direct apples-to-apples comparison

tasks.sourceNote: For 2021 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For scouting-interpretation tasks, verify game terms in official materials and support data claims with The Blue Alliance, Statbotics, team scouting resources, or clearly scoped community analysis.
tasks.publicExplanation: Tests awareness that data collection context matters.

tasks.prompt: Why might a mechanism that performs well in a controlled at-home-style challenge still need extra validation before being trusted for an in-person FRC event?
tasks.expectedAnswer: It may face different traffic, defense, field tolerances, cycle pressure, driver stress, inspection constraints, and repair demands at live events.

rubric.point: Mentions traffic, defense, or live-match interaction
Mentions field tolerance or environmental variation
Mentions driver stress, inspection, repair, or cycle pressure

tasks.sourceNote: For 2021 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For design-tradeoff tasks, verify game-objective terminology in the official manual. Support meta/design claims with team technical resources, public build blogs, event data, or community discussion when available.
tasks.publicExplanation: Tests whether the model separates controlled tests from event performance.

tasks.prompt: Which statement is safest for a benchmark about 2021 historical meta?
tasks.expectedAnswer: D
choices: A. Every region had the same strategic meta as a normal season
B. At-home scores are always equivalent to in-person playoff performance
C. Any online discussion is an official rule source
D. The benchmark should explain the data context and cite official challenge materials or community sources appropriately

rubric.point: Selects D

tasks.sourceNote: For 2021 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For historical-meta tasks, separate official season facts from retrospective community interpretation. Use official materials for rules and TBA/Statbotics/Chief Delphi/team resources for event or meta context.

tasks.prompt: In a 2022 playoff picklist, a team has strong scoring bursts but sometimes gets trapped in traffic. Another team scores less but drives clean defense and rarely draws fouls. Explain the picklist decision.
tasks.expectedAnswer: A good answer should compare alliance needs, marginal scoring, traffic tolerance, defense value, foul risk, reliability, and how the choice affects likely playoff matchups.

rubric.point: Frames the decision around alliance needs and marginal value
Considers traffic, defense, or matchup effects
Considers foul risk or reliability

tasks.sourceNote: For 2022 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. For alliance-selection claims, verify game constraints in official materials and support meta claims with event/picklist evidence from The Blue Alliance, Statbotics, Chief Delphi recaps, or public team strategy resources.
tasks.publicExplanation: Tests role fit and playoff matchup reasoning.

tasks.prompt: A qualification alliance is behind early but still has a path to a ranking objective. What should the strategist consider before pivoting away from pure win-chasing?
tasks.expectedAnswer: They should consider the probability of the ranking objective, remaining time, alliance capability, opponent strength, event ranking context, and whether the pivot still preserves a realistic win path.

rubric.point: Mentions ranking-objective probability
Mentions time remaining or alliance capability
Mentions win path or opponent strength

tasks.prompt: A 2022-style match log shows long cycle times after missed shots or failed pickups. What should scouts or strategists review?
tasks.expectedAnswer: They should review pickup reliability, recovery behavior after misses, driver pathing, defended versus undefended cycle times, shot selection, and whether partners caused congestion.

rubric.point: Mentions pickup reliability or recovery after misses
Mentions pathing, cycle time, or congestion
Mentions defense, shot selection, or partner effects

tasks.sourceNote: For 2022 tasks, verify season terminology against official FIRST materials before adding exact rule or scoring claims. tasks.prompt: A model is asked whether a specific 2022 defensive interaction is a foul, but no manual excerpt or update is provided. What should it do?
tasks.expectedAnswer: C
choices: A. Say it is legal because defense is always legal
B. Say it is illegal and invent a rule citation
C. Explain that the exact ruling needs the official manual or update, then describe what facts would matter
D. Ignore the rule question

rubric.point: Selects C

Identifies jams or reliability as risks

tasks.prompt: In a 2023-style strategy discussion, a robot is fast at one common scoring action but weak at endgame balancing. What alliance role and scouting data should be considered?
tasks.expectedAnswer: It may still be valuable as a cycling/scoring specialist if partners cover endgame needs. Check scoring consistency, cycle time, autonomous contribution, fouls, defense resistance, and endgame reliability.

rubric.point: Identifies a cycling/scoring specialist or role fit
Mentions partner coverage of the weak endgame
Mentions scouting metrics such as consistency, auto, fouls, or defense

tasks.prompt: During qualifications, an alliance can spend late-match time improving a ranking objective or protecting a narrow lead. What should it weigh?
tasks.expectedAnswer: It should weigh win probability, ranking-objective probability, current ranking context, partner capabilities, opponent scoring threat, and execution risk.

rubric.point: Mentions win probability or protecting the lead
Mentions ranking-objective probability or ranking context

tasks.sourceNote: For 2025 tasks, use official REEFSCAPE materials for game terminology before relying on community meta. For match-analysis tasks, verify field and scoring terminology in the official manual. Use The Blue Alliance match videos/data or team review resources when making concrete event claims.

tasks.prompt: For a 2025 build-season design review, a team can specialize in one task or build a more complex robot with broader capability. What should a strong answer include?
tasks.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should discuss role value, mechanism reliability, cycle time, software complexity, driver practice, integration risk, event meta uncertainty, partner compatibility, and repairability.

rubric.point: Mentions role value, specialization, or broad capability
Mentions reliability, cycle time, software, or integration risk
Mentions practice, meta uncertainty, partner compatibility, or repairability

tasks.sourceNote: For 2025 tasks, use official REEFSCAPE materials for game terminology before relying on community meta. For design-tradeoff tasks, verify game-objective terminology in the official manual. Support meta/design claims with team technical resources, public build blogs, event data, or community discussion when available.
tasks.publicExplanation: Tests build-scope reasoning for a recent season.
